sub: Technology block (b): handling thousands of live drone tracks without a thousand threads

title: Why Reactive?
desc: A non-blocking, asynchronous programming model for reactive applications

body.alt: Comparison of Spring MVC servlet stack (blocking) and Spring WebFlux reactive stack (non-blocking)

items: Efficiency — scales better than blocking frameworks: few event-loop threads instead of one thread per request
Reactive streams — large data volumes with minimal resources; event-driven — a natural fit for microservices and cloud
Part of Spring WebFlux, built on the Reactor core (Flux, Mono)
Air traffic reality check: 1,000 drones × 1 position/second = a permanent stream — exactly the reactive sweet spot

title: The Reactive Streams Specification
desc: Four interfaces and one crucial idea: backpressure

body.alt: Reactive streams: subscriber subscribes to publisher, subscription mediates request(n) and onNext/onComplete/onError

items: Publisher produces data, Subscriber consumes it, the Subscription mediates
The subscriber controls the flow: request(n) — 'send me at most n elements'
This is backpressure: a slow consumer never gets flooded by a fast producer

title: Core Concepts: Mono & Flux
desc: Two publisher types cover every API you will ever write

body.code: // Mono: 0 or 1 element — a single drone's state Mono<DroneState> state = repository.findById(droneId); // Flux: 0..N elements — the live track stream Flux<TrackUpdate> live = trackService.stream() .filter(t -> t.altitude() < 120) // open category only .sample(Duration.ofSeconds(1)) // throttle to 1 Hz .doOnNext(alertService::checkConflicts);

items: Mono — represents 0 or 1 element; Flux — represents an unbounded number of elements
WebFlux router & handlers — functional alternative to Spring MVC controllers (serve live above as a text/event-stream endpoint)

title: Tools, Libraries & When to Use It

rows: The reactive foundation under Spring WebFlux
Effortless integration of WebFlux into applications
Reactive database connectivity — replaces blocking JDBC

items: Spring Reactive suits modern, scalable applications
Ideal for microservices, streaming data and high load — i.e. telemetry ingestion, live track distribution, conflict alerting
Not a silver bullet: for simple CRUD services, classic blocking Spring MVC remains simpler to write and debug
